title: Singular spin fluctuations in the strange-metal phase of La2-xSrxCuO4
authors: B. Costarella, L. Soriano, I. Vinograd, H. Mayaffre, S. Li, J. Yang, J. Luo, R. Zhou, J. Yao, G. Gu, Q. Li, J. M. Tranquada, M. -H. Julien
abstract: Although spin fluctuations are central to the physics of high-Tc cuprates, their relevance to strange-metal behavior in the overdoped regime remains unclear. Here, we use high magnetic fields to suppress superconductivity and an NMR protocol tailored to electronic inhomogeneity to show that the low-energy limit of the dynamical spin susceptibility χ''(q,omega) at x=0.25 in La2-xSrxCuO4 increases continuously down to our lowest temperatures. This behavior is suggestive of quantum-critical fluctuations, a leading candidate mechanism for strange-metal transport, yet is observed well beyond the spin-stripe critical doping x=0.19. Our data further reveal that the spin dynamics are spatially inhomogeneous, suggesting that nanoscale electronic inhomogeneity may underlie this apparent paradox. These observations provide new insight into the electronic state from which strange-metal behavior emerges.
summary: The researchers used high-field NMR to show that singular spin fluctuations persist deep into the overdoped regime of LSCO, potentially explaining the origin of strange-metal transport. They also demonstrate that these fluctuations are spatially inhomogeneous, which may reconcile their presence far from the known stripe-order boundary. This provides a new perspective on the electronic state from which strange-metal behavior emerges.
main_problem: Determining whether singular spin fluctuations are the microscopic origin of strange-metal behavior in the overdoped regime of cuprates and resolving the paradox of their persistence beyond the spin-stripe critical doping.
main_result: The study finds that low-energy dynamical spin susceptibility increases continuously down to the lowest temperatures at x=0.25, suggesting quantum-critical-like fluctuations, while also revealing that spatial electronic inhomogeneity may drive this behavior.
method: High-field (up to 26 T) 139La NMR spectroscopy was used to suppress superconductivity and probe the intrinsic normal state, providing a more reliable measurement than 63Cu NMR which suffers from signal wipeout.
model_or_system: Overdoped La2-xSrxCuO4 (LSCO) single crystals at x=0.25, specifically investigating the strange-metal phase characterized by linear-in-temperature resistivity.
key_observables: Spin-lattice relaxation rate (1/T1T), dynamical spin susceptibility (chi''(q, omega)), stretching exponent (beta), and NMR lineshape broadening.
important_parameters: Hole-doping level p=0.25, magnetic field up to 26 T, temperature down to 1.34 K, and the spin-stripe critical doping x=0.19.
main_assumptions: Assumes that 139La NMR provides a representative probe of the bulk spin dynamics and that the observed 1/T1T increase is not an artifact of the density of states (Van Hove singularity).
figures_summary: Fig 1a shows 1/T1T fits to Curie-Weiss and power-law forms; Fig 1b shows the stretching exponent beta; Fig 4 shows the dependence of 63Cu 1/T1T on delay time tau; Fig 5 shows various fits for 139La 1/T1T; Fig 6 shows static inhomogeneity via NMR lineshape.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of strange-metal transport, details the experimental NMR methodology and the advantages of 139La over 63Cu, presents the discovery of singular spin fluctuations, discusses the role of electronic inhomogeneity, and concludes with implications for the quantum critical origin of the phase.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Evidence of singular behavior and spatial inhomo- geneity. (a) 139La 1/T1T at 26 T. Solid and dashed lines are fits to Curie–Weiss and power-law forms, respectively, each including a constant term (shown as colored background), as described in the text. Both fits are consistent with a diver- gence of χ′′(q, ω)/ω as T →0. (b) Stretching exponent β obtained from fits of the recovery curves (see Supplementary Information), introduced to phenomenologically account for the distribution of T1 values. A homogeneous system corre- sponds to β = 1. Dashed lines guide the eye.

FIG. 2. (a) Comparison of 139La 1/T1T measured at 20 and 26 T. The two data sets are nearly identical within experimental uncertainty (of the order of the symbol size). (b) 139La 1/T1T at 15 T. At this field the power-law fit becomes poor (not shown), whereas a Curie–Weiss fit (Eq. 2) yields a Weiss temperature θ = −20 ± 3 K, significantly larger than θ = −6.6 ± 2 K at 20 T and θ = −4.6 ± 0.9 K at 26 T. (c) 63Cu 1/T1T at 15 T compared with data from Itoh et al. [48] taken at 5 T (both with B ∥ab). The low-field data show a drop at low temperature due to the opening of the superconducting gap. The solid line is a Curie–Weiss fit yielding a larger Weiss temperature θ = −45 ± 23 K and a...

FIG. 3. a, 139La nuclear magnetization recovery after an inversion pulse (T = 4 K, B = 26 T). The better fit with the stretching exponent β < 1 is evidence of a distribution of T1 values. b, Temperature dependence of the stretching exponent β at different magnetic field values (data at 26 T are identical to those in Fig. 1b). Inhomogeneity becomes noticeable below approximately 100 K and is remarkably field independent. c, Temperature dependence of the two T1 values obtained from a two-component fit of the recoveries (the fit is practically indistinguishable from the stretched fit in (a) and is thus not shown). The varying symbol size schematically reflects the varying weight of the two...

FIG. 4. Effect of fast, inhomogeneous T2 on 63Cu and 139La T1 measurements. (a) Dependence of 63Cu 1/T1 on the delay time τ between the two radio-frequency pulses of the NMR spin-echo sequence, measured at 40 and 25 K. (b) Temperature dependence of 63Cu 1/T1T for different values of τ. Open symbols correspond to the τ →0 limit of the data in panel (a). For τ = 16 µs, 1/T1T stays flat between 40 and 25 K—similar to the data of ref. [28]—but increases upon cooling for τ = 4 µs as in the data of ref. [48]. The average T2 becomes so short that the NMR signal eventually disappears below 19 K, even for τ as short as 4 µs—the minimum accessible with our spectrometer. (c) Dependence of 139La 1/T1...
FIG. 5. Various fits, all of comparable quality, to the temperature dependence of 139La 1/T1T at 26 T.
